title: Find bridges Tarjan
description: Find bridges in graph using Tarjan

examples.explanation: Step 1: Graph has edges (0,1), (1,2), (2,3), (3,0). Step 2: Test edge (0,1): Remove it temporarily. Adjacency: 0: [3], 1: [2], 2: [1,3], 3: [2,0]. Run DFS from 0: visit 0, then 3, then 2, then 1. All 4 vertices visited, so (0,1) is not a bridge. Restore edge. Step 3: Test edge (1,2): Remove it. Adjacency: 0: [1,3], 1: [0], 2: [3], 3: [2,0]. DFS from 0: visit 0, then 1, then 3, then 2. All visited, not a bridge. Restore. Step 4: Test edge (2,3): Remove it. Adjacency: 0: [1,3], 1: [0,2], 2: [1], 3: [0]. DFS from 0: visit 0, then 1, then 2, then 3. All visited, not a bridge. Restore. Step 5: Test edge (3,0): Remove it. Adjacency: 0: [1], 1: [0,2], 2: [1,3], 3: [2]. DFS from 0: visit 0, then 1, then 2, then 3. All visited, not a bridge. Restore. Step 6: No bridges found, output [].

Step 1: Graph is a path: 0-1-2-3-4. Step 2: Test edge (0,1): Remove it. Adjacency: 0: [], 1: [2], 2: [1,3], 3: [2,4], 4: [3]. DFS from 0: visit only 0. Reachable vertices = 1 < 5, so (0,1) is a bridge. Restore. Step 3: Test edge (1,2): Remove it. Adjacency: 0: [1], 1: [0], 2: [3], 3: [2,4], 4: [3]. DFS from 0: visit 0 and 1. Reachable = 2 < 5, bridge. Restore. Step 4: Test edge (2,3): Remove it. Adjacency: 0: [1], 1: [0,2], 2: [1], 3: [4], 4: [3]. DFS from 0: visit 0,1,2. Reachable = 3 < 5, bridge. Restore. Step 5: Test edge (3,4): Remove it. Adjacency: 0: [1], 1: [0,2], 2: [1,3], 3: [2], 4: []. DFS from 0: visit 0,1,2,3. Reachable = 4 < 5, bridge. Restore. Step 6: Bridges are all edges: [[0,1], [1,2], [2,3], [3,4]].

approaches.name: Brute Force Edge Removal
approaches.summary: Iterate over all edges, temporarily remove each edge, and run DFS/BFS to check if the graph becomes disconnected. Use this for small graphs or as a baseline to understand bridge properties.

ladders.title: Full Problem — Brute Force Edge Removal
ladders.desc: Complete implementation of brute force bridge detection by iteratively removing edges and checking connectivity.
ladders.explanation: The brute force approach to finding bridges in an undirected graph works by systematically testing each edge to see if its removal disconnects the graph. The intuition is that a bridge is an edge whose removal increases the number of connected components. For each edge (u, v) in the graph, we temporarily remove it, then perform a DFS/BFS from an arbitrary vertex to see if all vertices remain reachable. If not, (u, v) is a bridge. This method is straightforward but inefficient, making it suitable only for small graphs or as a conceptual baseline. The algorithm: 1) Represent the graph as an adjacency list. 2) For each edge (u, v): a) Temporarily remove (u, v) from the adjacency lists of u and v. b) Run DFS/BFS from vertex 0 (or any vertex) to count reachable vertices. c) If reachable vertices < total vertices, (u, v) is a bridge. d) Restore the edge. Time complexity is O(E*(V+E)) because for each of E edges, we run DFS/BFS taking O(V+E) time. Space complexity is O(V+E) for storing the graph and DFS/BFS data structures. This connects to understanding bridge properties but is impractical for large graphs. Common mistakes include not properly restoring edges after testing (leading to corrupted graph state) and assuming the graph is connected initially (handle disconnected graphs by checking each component). Real code pattern: Use adjacency list, iterate edges, modify lists temporarily, run DFS to check connectivity, and collect bridges.

examples.trace:
Step 1: Graph has edges (0,1), (1,2), (2,3), (3,0). Step 2: Test edge (0,1): Remove it temporarily. Adjacency: 0: [3], 1: [2], 2: [1,3], 3: [2,0]. Run DFS from 0: visit 0, then 3, then 2, then 1. All 4 vertices visited, so (0,1) is not a bridge. Restore edge. Step 3: Test edge (1,2): Remove it. Adjacency: 0: [1,3], 1: [0], 2: [3], 3: [2,0]. DFS from 0: visit 0, then 1, then 3, then 2. All visited, not a bridge. Restore. Step 4: Test edge (2,3): Remove it. Adjacency: 0: [1,3], 1: [0,2], 2: [1], 3: [0]. DFS from 0: visit 0, then 1, then 2, then 3
... (simplified)

Step 1: Graph is a path: 0-1-2-3-4. Step 2: Test edge (0,1): Remove it. Adjacency: 0: [], 1: [2], 2: [1,3], 3: [2,4], 4: [3]. DFS from 0: visit only 0. Reachable vertices = 1 < 5, so (0,1) is a bridge. Restore. Step 3: Test edge (1,2): Remove it. Adjacency: 0: [1], 1: [0], 2: [3], 3: [2,4], 4: [3]. DFS from 0: visit 0 and 1. Reachable = 2 < 5, bridge. Restore. Step 4: Test edge (2,3): Remove it. Adjacency: 0: [1], 1: [0,2], 2: [1], 3: [4], 4: [3]. DFS from 0: visit 0,1,2. Reachable = 3 < 5, bridge. Restore. Step 5: Test edge (3,4): Remove it. A
... (simplified)

ladders.title: Key Sub-routine — Connectivity Check / DFS with Low-Link
ladders.desc: Implement DFS/BFS to determine if a graph remains connected when a specific edge is removed.
ladders.explanation: This sub-routine is the core of the brute force approach: given a graph and an edge to test, temporarily remove that edge and check if the graph stays connected. The intuition is that connectivity is preserved if and only if all vertices can be reached from any starting vertex after edge removal. We use DFS (or BFS) to traverse the graph from an arbitrary vertex (e.g., vertex 0) and count how many vertices are visited. If the count equals the total number of vertices, the graph is connected; otherwise, it's disconnected, indicating the edge is a bridge. Steps: 1) Create a copy of the adjacency list or modify it in place by removing the edge (u, v) from both u's and v's lists. 2) Initialize a visited array of size V, all false. 3) Start DFS from vertex 0: mark as visited, recursively visit all unvisited neighbors. 4) After DFS, count visited vertices. 5) Restore the edge by adding it back to the adjacency lists. Time complexity is O(V+E) for the DFS traversal. Space complexity is O(V) for the visited array and recursion stack. This feeds into the full problem by being called for each edge. Common mistakes include not handling disconnected initial graphs (start DFS from each component, but here we assume connected or check from one vertex; if graph is initially disconnected, bridges are defined within components). Real code pattern: Use recursion or stack for DFS, modify adjacency lists temporarily, and compare visited count to V.

examples.trace: Step 1: Original adjacency: 0:[1,3], 1:[0,2], 2:[1,3], 3:[2,0]. Step 2: Remove edge (0,1): new adjacency: 0:[3], 1:[2], 2:[1,3], 3:[2,0]. Step 3: Start DFS from vertex 0. Mark 0 visited. Step 4: From 0, neighbor 3 is unvisited, go to 3. Mark 3 visited. Step 5: From 3, neighbor 2 is unvisited, go to 2. Mark 2 visited. Step 6: From 2, neighbor 1 is unvisited, go to 1. Mark 1 visited. Step 7: All vertices visited (count=4). Graph is connected. Step 8: Restore edge (0,1).

Step 1: Original adjacency: 0:[1], 1:[0,2], 2:[1,3], 3:[2,4], 4:[3]. Step 2: Remove edge (1,2): new adjacency: 0:[1], 1:[0], 2:[3], 3:[2,4], 4:[3]. Step 3: Start DFS from vertex 0. Mark 0 visited. Step 4: From 0, neighbor 1 is unvisited, go to 1. Mark 1 visited. Step 5: From 1, no unvisited neighbors (only 0 is visited). DFS stops. Step 6: Visited vertices: 0 and 1 (count=2). Total vertices=5, so graph is disconnected. Step 7: Restore edge (1,2).

ladders.title: Core Logic — Bridge Detection Condition
ladders.desc: The central technique of testing each edge individually to identify bridges.
ladders.explanation: The core logic of the brute force approach is the iterative process of testing every edge in the graph to determine if it is a bridge. The intuition is that a bridge is defined by its property: removing it disconnects the graph. By testing each edge one by one, we can directly verify this property. This method relies on the definition of bridges and does not require advanced algorithms like Tarjan's, making it conceptually simple but computationally expensive. The algorithm: 1) List all edges in the graph. 2) For each edge (u, v) in the list: a) Temporarily remove (u, v) from the graph representation. b) Check if the graph remains connected (using DFS/BFS). c) If disconnected, add (u, v) to the list of bridges. d) Restore (u, v) to the graph. This logic is central because it directly implements the bridge definition. Time complexity arises from repeating the connectivity check for each edge. Space complexity is O(V+E) for the graph storage. This feeds into the key sub-routine by providing the loop structure that calls the connectivity check. Common mistakes include modifying the original graph permanently (always restore edges) and not considering parallel edges or self-loops (handle them appropriately; in this context, bridges are defined for simple undirected graphs). Real code pattern: Use a for-loop over edges, call connectivity function, and accumulate results.

examples.trace: Step 1: Edge list: (0,1), (1,2), (2,0). Step 2: Test edge (0,1): Remove it, check connectivity. Graph becomes: edges (1,2),(2,0). DFS from 0 visits all vertices (0,2,1), so connected. Not a bridge. Restore. Step 3: Test edge (1,2): Remove it, graph: (0,1),(2,0). DFS from 0 visits all (0,1,2), connected. Not a bridge. Restore. Step 4: Test edge (2,0): Remove it, graph: (0,1),(1,2). DFS from 0 visits all (0,1,2), connected. Not a bridge. Restore. Step 5: No bridges found.

Step 1: Edge list: (0,1), (1,2), (2,3). Step 2: Test edge (0,1): Remove it, graph: (1,2),(2,3). DFS from 0 visits only 0, disconnected. Bridge found: (0,1). Restore. Step 3: Test edge (1,2): Remove it, graph: (0,1),(2,3). DFS from 0 visits 0 and 1, disconnected. Bridge: (1,2). Restore. Step 4: Test edge (2,3): Remove it, graph: (0,1),(1,2). DFS from 0 visits 0,1,2, disconnected (3 not visited). Bridge: (2,3). Restore. Step 5: Bridges: all edges.

ladders.title: Building Block — Graph Representation
ladders.desc: Represent a graph using adjacency lists and implement temporary edge removal.
ladders.explanation: This building block involves representing an undirected graph in a way that allows efficient edge removal and restoration. The intuition is that to test an edge for being a bridge, we need to modify the graph temporarily without affecting subsequent tests. We use an adjacency list: an array of lists where index i contains neighbors of vertex i. For an undirected edge (u, v), we add v to u's list and u to v's list. To remove an edge temporarily, we delete v from u's list and u from v's list. After testing, we restore it by adding them back. This representation is chosen because it allows O(degree) edge removal and restoration, which is efficient for sparse graphs. Steps: 1) Initialize adjacency list of size V with empty lists. 2) For each edge (u, v), append v to adj[u] and u to adj[v]. 3) To remove edge (u, v): find and remove v from adj[u] and u from adj[v] (using list removal or marking). 4) To restore: append back if removed. Time complexity for building is O(V+E). Space is O(V+E). This feeds into core logic by providing the mutable graph structure. Common mistakes include not handling duplicate edges (in simple graphs, assume no duplicates) or altering the original adjacency list permanently (use copies or careful restoration). Real code pattern: Use list of lists in Python, with methods like remove() or pop() for deletion.

examples.trace: Step 1: Build adjacency: 0:[1,2], 1:[0,2], 2:[1,0]. Step 2: Remove edge (0,1): From adj[0], remove 1 → becomes [2]. From adj[1], remove 0 → becomes [2]. Step 3: Adjacency after: 0:[2], 1:[2], 2:[1,0]. Step 4: To restore, add 1 to adj[0] and 0 to adj[1].

Step 1: Build adjacency: 0:[1], 1:[0,2], 2:[1,3], 3:[2]. Step 2: Remove edge (1,2): From adj[1], remove 2 → becomes [0]. From adj[2], remove 1 → becomes [3]. Step 3: Adjacency after: 0:[1], 1:[0], 2:[3], 3:[2]. Step 4: Restore by adding back.

ladders.title: Basic Operation — DFS Traversal / Edge Testing
ladders.desc: Implement DFS to visit all vertices in a graph from a starting point.
ladders.explanation: DFS is a fundamental graph traversal algorithm used to explore all vertices reachable from a starting vertex. The intuition is to go as deep as possible along each branch before backtracking, which ensures we visit all connected vertices. In the context of bridge detection, DFS helps check connectivity by counting visited vertices. Steps: 1) Initialize a visited array of size V, set all to false. 2) Define a recursive function dfs(u): mark u as visited, then for each neighbor v of u, if v is not visited, call dfs(v). 3) Start from a given vertex (e.g., 0). 4) After traversal, count how many vertices are visited. For iterative implementation, use a stack. Time complexity is O(V+E) as each vertex and edge is processed once. Space complexity is O(V) for visited array and recursion stack (O(V) worst-case for a path graph). This feeds into graph connectivity checks. Common mistakes include not marking visited before recursion (causing infinite loops) or assuming the graph is directed (use undirected neighbors). Real code pattern: Use recursion with a helper function, iterate over adjacency list.

examples.trace: Step 1: Start at 0, mark visited. Step 2: From 0, neighbors 1 and 3. Choose 1 (unvisited), go to 1. Mark 1 visited. Step 3: From 1, neighbors 0 and 2. 0 visited, so go to 2. Mark 2 visited. Step 4: From 2, neighbors 1 and 3. 1 visited, so go to 3. Mark 3 visited. Step 5: From 3, neighbors 2 and 0, both visited. Backtrack to 2, then 1, then 0. All vertices visited.

Step 1: Start at 0, mark visited. Step 2: From 0, neighbor 1, go to 1. Mark 1 visited. Step 3: From 1, neighbors 0 and 2. 0 visited, go to 2. Mark 2 visited. Step 4: From 2, neighbor 1 visited. Backtrack. Step 5: Vertices 3 and 4 are not visited because they are in a separate component.

ladders.title: Concept Foundation — Bridges & DFS
ladders.desc: Basic understanding of bridges and DFS traversal in undirected graphs.
ladders.explanation: A bridge is an edge whose removal increases the number of connected components. Tarjan's algorithm uses DFS with discovery times and low-link values to find bridges efficiently in O(V+E) time.

examples.output: Connected: yes, Bridges: edges (0,1) and (1,2) are bridges
examples.trace: Step 1: Vertices: 0,1,2. Edges: 0-1 and 1-2. Step 2: Check connectivity: Path from 0 to 2 via 0-1-2, so graph is connected. Step 3: Check if edge (0,1) is a bridge: Remove it, graph becomes edges [1-2]. Now no path from 0 to 1 or 2, so disconnected. Thus (0,1) is a bridge. Step 4: Similarly, (1,2) is a bridge.

Step 1: Vertices: 0,1,2,3. Edges form a cycle: 0-1-2-3-0. Step 2: Connectivity: There are paths between all vertices (e.g., 0 to 2 via 0-1-2 or 0-3-2). Step 3: Check edge (0,1): Remove it, graph has edges 1-2, 2-3, 3-0. Path from 0 to 1 via 0-3-2-1, so still connected. Not a bridge. Step 4: Similarly, no edge is a bridge.

approaches.name: Tarjan's DFS Low-Link
approaches.summary: Perform a DFS traversal, assigning discovery times and low-link values to vertices; an edge is a bridge if the low-link value of the destination is greater than the discovery time of the source. This is the standard efficient method for finding bridges in undirected graphs.

ladders.title: Full Problem — Tarjan's DFS Low-Link
ladders.desc: Implement Tarjan's algorithm to find all bridge edges in an undirected graph.
ladders.explanation:
Tarjan's algorithm for finding bridges is a DFS-based approach that efficiently identifies edges whose removal increases the number of connected components. The intuition is that during DFS traversal, we track two values for each vertex: discovery time (when it was first visited) and low-link value (the earliest discovery time reachable from that vertex including back edges). A bridge exists when the low-link value of the destination vertex is greater than the discovery time of the source vertex—this means there's no alternative path connecting the two components separated by that edge.

Step-by-step algorithm:
1. Initialize arrays: disc[] for discovery times, low[] for low-link values, visited[] to track visited vertices, parent[] to track parent in DFS tree, and time counter.
2. Perform DFS from each unvisited vertex (handling disconnected graphs).
3. For each vertex u:
   - Mark visited, set disc[u] = low[u] = ++time
   - For each neighbor v:
     - If v is not visited: set parent[v] = u, recursively DFS(v), then update low[u] = min(low[u], low[v])
     - If low[v] > disc[u], edge (u,v) is a bridge
     - If v is visited and v ≠ parent[u], update low[u] = min(low[u], disc[v]) (back edge)
4. Collect all bridge edges.

Time complexity: O(V+E) where V is vertices, E is edges (each vertex/edge processed once). Space complexity: O(V+E) for adjacency list and arrays.

Connection to L1: This full solution integrates the DFS traversal with bridge detection logic.

Common mistakes:
1. Forgetting to handle disconnected graphs (need to DFS from all unvisited vertices).
2. Incorrect back edge condition (must exclude parent to avoid immediate back edge).
3. Not updating low values correctly after recursive calls.

Real code pattern:
def find_bridges(graph, n):
    disc = [-1]*n
    low = [-1]*n
    visited = [False]*n
    parent = [-1]*n
    time = 0
    bridges = []
    
    def dfs(u):
        nonlocal time
        visited[u] = True
        disc[u] = low[u] = time
        time += 1
        
        for v in graph[u]:
            if not visited[v]:
                parent[v] = u
                dfs(v)
                low[u] = min(low[u], low[v])
                if low[v] > disc[u]:
                    bridges.append((u,v))
            elif v != parent[u]:
                low[u] = min(low[u], disc[v])
    
    for i in range(n):
        if not visited[i]:
            dfs(i)
    return bridges

examples.trace: Graph: 0-1-2-3 (linear chain). DFS from 0: time=0, disc[0]=0, low[0]=0. Visit neighbor 1: time=1, disc[1]=1, low[1]=1. From 1 visit 2: time=2, disc[2]=2, low[2]=2. From 2 visit 3: time=3, disc[3]=3, low[3]=3. Backtrack from 3: low[2]=min(2,3)=2, check low[3]=3 > disc[2]=2 → bridge (2,3). Backtrack from 2: low[1]=min(1,2)=1, check low[2]=2 > disc[1]=1 → bridge (1,2). Backtrack from 1: low[0]=min(0,1)=0, check low[1]=1 > disc[0]=0 → bridge (0,1). All edges are bridges.

Graph with cycle 0-1-2-0 and branch 1-3-4. DFS from 0: disc[0]=0, low[0]=0. Visit 1: disc[1]=1, low[1]=1. From 1 visit 2: disc[2]=2, low[2]=2. From 2, neighbor 0 is visited and not parent (parent[2]=1), so low[2]=min(2, disc[0]=0)=0. Backtrack: low[1]=min(1, low[2]=0)=0. From 1 visit 3: disc[3]=3, low[3]=3. From 3 visit 4: disc[4]=4, low[4]=4. Backtrack: check low[4]=4 > disc[3]=3 → bridge (3,4). Update low[3]=min(3,4)=3. Backtrack to 1: check low[3]=3 > disc[1]=1 → bridge (1,3). Update low[1]=min(0,3)=0. Backtrack to 0: low[0]=min(0,0)=0. Edge (0,1) not bridge since low[1]=0 ≤ disc[0]=0.

ladders.title: Key Sub-routine — Connectivity Check / DFS with Low-Link
ladders.desc: Implement DFS that tracks discovery times and computes low-link values for vertices.
ladders.explanation:
This sub-routine is the core DFS traversal in Tarjan's algorithm. The intuition is that by performing DFS, we create a spanning tree of the graph, and by tracking discovery times and low-link values, we capture connectivity information. Discovery time (disc[u]) records when vertex u was first visited in DFS order. Low-link value (low[u]) represents the earliest discovery time reachable from u via its descendants and back edges—this helps identify whether alternative paths exist around edges.

Step-by-step:
1. Initialize arrays: disc[] initialized to -1 (unvisited), low[] to -1, visited boolean array, parent array.
2. For vertex u:
   - Mark visited, set disc[u] = low[u] = current time, increment time.
   - For each neighbor v:
     - If v not visited: set parent[v]=u, recursively DFS(v), then update low[u] = min(low[u], low[v])
     - If v visited and v ≠ parent[u]: update low[u] = min(low[u], disc[v]) (back edge case)

Time complexity: O(V+E) as standard DFS. Space: O(V) for arrays plus O(V) recursion stack.

Connection to L0: This DFS provides the disc and low values needed for bridge detection in the full problem.

Common mistakes:
1. Not initializing disc/low to -1 for unvisited vertices.
2. Forgetting to exclude parent when checking visited neighbors for back edges.
3. Incorrect update order (must update low[u] after recursive DFS(v)).

Real code pattern:
def dfs_tarjan(u, graph, disc, low, visited, parent, time):
    visited[u] = True
    disc[u] = low[u] = time[0]
    time[0] += 1
    
    for v in graph[u]:
        if not visited[v]:
            parent[v] = u
            dfs_tarjan(v, graph, disc, low, visited, parent, time)
            low[u] = min(low[u], low[v])
        elif v != parent[u]:
            low[u] = min(low[u], disc[v])

examples.trace: Initialize: disc=[-1,-1,-1], low=[-1,-1,-1], time=0. DFS(0): visited[0]=True, disc[0]=0, low[0]=0, time=1. Neighbor 1 not visited: parent[1]=0, DFS(1): disc[1]=1, low[1]=1, time=2. Neighbor 2 not visited: parent[2]=1, DFS(2): disc[2]=2, low[2]=2, time=3. Backtrack from 2: low[1]=min(1,2)=1. Backtrack from 1: low[0]=min(0,1)=0. Final: disc=[0,1,2], low=[0,1,2].

examples.input: Start from vertex 0, graph: triangle 0-1-2-0, edges: [[0,1],[1,2],[2,0]]
examples.trace: Initialize arrays size 3. DFS(0): disc[0]=0, low[0]=0, time=1. Visit neighbor 1: disc[1]=1, low[1]=1, time=2. From 1 visit 2: disc[2]=2, low[2]=2, time=3. From 2, neighbor 0 is visited and not parent (parent[2]=1), so low[2]=min(2, disc[0]=0)=0. Backtrack: low[1]=min(1, low[2]=0)=0. From 1, neighbor 0 visited and is parent (parent[1]=0) so skip. Backtrack: low[0]=min(0, low[1]=0)=0. Final: disc=[0,1,2], low=[0,0,0].

ladders.title: Core Logic — Bridge Detection Condition
ladders.desc: Determine if an edge is a bridge based on discovery time of source and low-link value of destination.
ladders.explanation:
This is the central insight of Tarjan's algorithm: an edge (u,v) where u is parent of v in DFS tree is a bridge if and only if low[v] > disc[u]. The intuition is that low[v] represents the earliest discovery time reachable from v (including through back edges). If low[v] is greater than disc[u], it means v cannot reach any vertex discovered before u—there's no alternative path connecting the component containing v back to u, so removing edge (u,v) would disconnect the graph.

Why this works:
- disc[u] is the time when u was discovered.
- low[v] is the minimum discovery time reachable from v.
- If low[v] ≤ disc[u], then v can reach u or some ancestor of u via a back edge, creating a cycle that makes (u,v) non-bridge.
- If low[v] > disc[u], the only connection from v to u's side is through edge (u,v) itself, making it critical.

Algorithm context:
During DFS, after processing child v recursively, check if low[v] > disc[u]. If true, edge (u,v) is a bridge.

Time complexity: O(1) per edge check. Space: O(1).

Connection to L1: This condition is applied during the DFS traversal after processing each child.

Common mistakes:
1. Checking condition before low[v] is fully computed (must be after recursive call).
2. Applying to wrong direction (only check when u is parent of v).
3. Misunderstanding inequality (strictly greater, not greater or equal).

Real code pattern:
# Inside DFS after recursive call to child v
low[u] = min(low[u], low[v])
if low[v] > disc[u]:
    # Edge (u,v) is a bridge

examples.trace: Given disc[1]=1, low[2]=2. Check condition: low[2]=2 > disc[1]=1 is true. This means vertex 2 cannot reach any vertex with discovery time ≤1. The only connection from component containing 2 to vertex 1 is through edge (1,2), so it's a bridge.

Given disc[0]=0, low[1]=0. Check: low[1]=0 > disc[0]=0 is false (0 is not greater than 0). This means vertex 1 can reach a vertex with discovery time 0 (vertex 0 itself or another vertex discovered at time 0). There exists an alternative path, so edge (0,1) is not a bridge.

ladders.title: Building Block — Graph Representation
ladders.desc: Calculate the low-link value for each vertex during DFS traversal.
ladders.explanation:
The low-link value of a vertex u (low[u]) is defined as the smallest discovery time of any vertex reachable from u through its descendants in the DFS tree, including back edges to ancestors. This concept is crucial because it captures connectivity information: a low value equal to an ancestor's discovery time indicates a cycle, while a higher low value suggests isolation.

Intuition: During DFS, we propagate low values upward. When we find a back edge from u to an ancestor v, we update low[u] to disc[v] (since u can reach v directly). When backtracking from child v to parent u, we update low[u] with low[v] (since u can reach whatever v can reach).

Step-by-step computation:
1. Initialize low[u] = disc[u] when first visiting u.
2. For each neighbor v of u:
   - If v is not visited (tree edge): after DFS(v), update low[u] = min(low[u], low[v])
   - If v is visited and v ≠ parent[u] (back edge): update low[u] = min(low[u], disc[v])

Why update with disc[v] for back edges? Because the back edge goes directly to v, whose discovery time is disc[v] (which could be smaller than low[u]).

Time complexity: O(degree(u)) per vertex. Space: O(1) extra.

Connection to L2: Low-link values are used in the bridge detection condition low[v] > disc[u].

Common mistakes:
1. Using low[v] instead of disc[v] for back edge updates (incorrect).
2. Not excluding parent when checking visited neighbors.
3. Updating low[u] before recursive call instead of after.

Real code pattern:
# Inside DFS for vertex u
for v in graph[u]:
    if not visited[v]:
        # Tree edge
        dfs(v)
        low[u] = min(low[u], low[v])
    elif v != parent[u]:
        # Back edge
        low[u] = min(low[u], disc[v])

examples.input: Vertex u=1 with disc[1]=3, neighbors: v=2 (tree edge, low[2]=2), v=0 (back edge, disc[0]=0)
examples.trace: Initialize low[1]=disc[1]=3. Process neighbor 2 (tree edge): after DFS(2), low[2]=2, so update low[1]=min(3,2)=2. Process neighbor 0 (back edge, and 0 ≠ parent[1]): disc[0]=0, so update low[1]=min(2,0)=0. Final low[1]=0.

examples.input: Vertex u=2 with disc[2]=5, neighbors: v=3 (tree edge, low[3]=6), v=1 (back edge, disc[1]=2)
examples.trace: Initialize low[2]=5. Process neighbor 3: low[3]=6, update low[2]=min(5,6)=5. Process neighbor 1 (back edge, 1 ≠ parent[2]): disc[1]=2, update low[2]=min(5,2)=2. Final low[2]=2.

testCases.input: disc[u]=4, initial low[u]=4, tree edges: low[child]=3
disc[u]=4, initial low[u]=4, tree edges: low[child]=5
disc[u]=4, initial low[u]=4, back edge: disc[neighbor]=2
disc[u]=4, initial low[u]=4, back edge: disc[neighbor]=6
disc[u]=4, initial low[u]=4, tree: low[child]=3, back: disc[neighbor]=1
disc[u]=4, initial low[u]=4, tree: low[child]=5, back: disc[neighbor]=3
disc[u]=7, initial low[u]=7, tree: low[child]=8, back: disc[neighbor]=6
disc[u]=7, initial low[u]=7, tree: low[child]=6, back: disc[neighbor]=7
disc[u]=7, initial low[u]=7, tree: low[child]=9, back: disc[neighbor]=9

ladders.title: Basic Operation — DFS Traversal / Edge Testing
ladders.desc: Perform depth-first search while assigning sequential discovery times to vertices.
ladders.explanation:
This is a fundamental graph traversal operation enhanced with timing information. Discovery time (disc[u]) is a counter that increments each time we visit a new vertex in DFS order. It serves as a unique timestamp for each vertex, establishing a temporal order of visitation that helps analyze graph structure.

Intuition: By recording when each vertex is discovered, we create a timeline of the DFS exploration. This allows us to determine ancestor-descendant relationships in the DFS tree (earlier discovery times are ancestors of later ones).

Algorithm:
1. Initialize disc array with -1 (unvisited), visited array False, time counter = 0.
2. For vertex u:
   - Mark visited[u] = True
   - Set disc[u] = time
   - Increment time by 1
   - For each neighbor v of u:
     - If not visited[v], recursively DFS(v)

Time complexity: O(V+E) for full graph traversal. Space: O(V) for arrays plus recursion stack.

Connection to L3: Discovery times provide the disc values needed for computing low-link values.

Common mistakes:
1. Not resetting time for different connected components.
2. Using disc as boolean visited instead of numeric timestamp.
3. Not incrementing time correctly (pre-increment vs post-increment).

Real code pattern:
def dfs_with_time(u, graph, disc, visited, time):
    visited[u] = True
    disc[u] = time[0]
    time[0] += 1
    
    for v in graph[u]:
        if not visited[v]:
            dfs_with_time(v, graph, disc, visited, time)

examples.trace: Initialize disc=[-1,-1,-1], visited=[False,False,False], time=0. DFS(0): visited[0]=True, disc[0]=0, time=1. Neighbor 1 not visited: DFS(1): disc[1]=1, time=2. Neighbor 2 not visited: DFS(2): disc[2]=2, time=3. Final disc=[0,1,2].

examples.input: Start from vertex 0, graph: star with center 0 connected to 1,2,3
examples.trace: Initialize arrays size 4. DFS(0): disc[0]=0, time=1. Process neighbors in order: neighbor 1 not visited: DFS(1): disc[1]=1, time=2. Back to 0: neighbor 2 not visited: DFS(2): disc[2]=2, time=3. Back to 0: neighbor 3 not visited: DFS(3): disc[3]=3, time=4. Final disc=[0,1,2,3] (order depends on neighbor processing order).

ladders.title: Concept Foundation — Bridges & DFS
ladders.desc: Basic understanding of bridges and DFS traversal in undirected graphs.
ladders.explanation: A bridge is an edge whose removal increases the number of connected components. Tarjan's algorithm uses DFS with discovery times and low-link values to find bridges efficiently in O(V+E) time.

examples.input: Start from vertex 0, graph: 0 connected to 1 and 2, 1 connected to 3
examples.output: Visited order: 0,1,3,2 (or 0,2,1,3 depending on neighbor order)
examples.trace: Initialize visited=[False,False,False,False]. DFS(0): mark visited[0]=True. Process neighbors: suppose order is 1 then 2. Neighbor 1 not visited: DFS(1): mark visited[1]=True. Neighbors of 1: 3 not visited: DFS(3): mark visited[3]=True. Backtrack to 1 (no more neighbors), backtrack to 0. Next neighbor 2 not visited: DFS(2): mark visited[2]=True. Final visited all true.

examples.input: Start from vertex 0, graph: two disconnected components: 0-1 and 2-3
examples.trace: Initialize visited size 4. DFS(0): mark visited[0]=True, visit neighbor 1: DFS(1): mark visited[1]=True. Backtrack, component 0-1 done. Main loop continues: vertex 2 not visited: DFS(2): mark visited[2]=True, visit neighbor 3: DFS(3): mark visited[3]=True. All vertices visited.
